name: San Fermín — Running of the Bulls
tagline: Nine days when a dignified Navarrese capital dresses in white and red and throws the wildest, most dangerous street party on earth.
dates: July 6–14, 2027 (fixed annual dates; encierros run July 7–14 at 8:00am)
history: San Fermín fuses two medieval traditions that collided in Pamplona centuries ago: early-summer commercial fairs, when cattle merchants drove their herds into the city and bullfights followed, and the religious feast of Saint Fermín — a 3rd-century convert who returned to Pamplona as its first bishop and died a martyr. In 1591 the city moved his October feast to July 7 to coincide with the fair, and the modern fiesta was born. The encierro itself grew from pure practicality: drovers running the fighting bulls from corrals outside the walls to the ring each morning, joined over time by young men who couldn't resist sprinting alongside.

Ernest Hemingway arrived in 1923 and returned repeatedly until 1959; his 1926 novel The Sun Also Rises turned a regional fiesta into a global pilgrimage, and Pamplona has never looked back — there's a bust of him outside the bullring and his table is still pointed out at Café Iruña. Today roughly a million visitors descend on a city of 200,000 for nine days. The rhythm is fixed: the chupinazo rocket fired from the town hall balcony at noon on July 6 (a tradition formalised in 1941) detonates the party, and at midnight on July 14 the crowd sings the mournful 'Pobre de Mí' by candlelight, removing their red scarves until next year.

The encierro is the festival's beating heart and its dark edge: six fighting bulls and six steers cover an 825-metre course of narrow cobbled streets — up Santo Domingo, across the town hall square, through the dead-man's curve at Mercaderes, up Calle Estafeta and into the bullring — in under three minutes. Since record-keeping began in 1925, 15 runners have died and 200–300 are injured each year, most in pile-ups rather than on the horns. Beyond the run, the days fill with the giants-and-big-heads parade (the eight four-metre giants date to 1860), 6pm bullfights, roaming peña brass bands, and fireworks over the citadel every night at 11.
whyGo: No other European festival compresses this much ritual, adrenaline and communal joy into one week — an entire city in identical white-and-red, brass bands at dawn, and a 3-minute morning event with genuine 400-year-old stakes. You don't have to run (most shouldn't) to feel the electricity of 8am on Estafeta. It falls in the traveler's final summer window (July 2027), making it a natural grand-finale trip.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Bilbao (BIO) direct with Vueling (~1h55), then ALSA/La Burundesa bus Bilbao → Pamplona
gettingThere.notes: Fastest realistic option. Bilbao–Pamplona buses run roughly hourly; book flights 3–4 months out — San Fermín week fares spike. Alternative gateway: ZRH → Barcelona, then Renfe Alvia to Pamplona (~4h rail).

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Paris Gare de Lyon (TGV Lyria, ~4h05), cross to Montparnasse, TGV → Hendaye/San Sebastián (~4h30), then bus or train to Pamplona (~1h–1h15)
gettingThere.duration: 11–13h; overnight in San Sebastián works well
gettingThere.notes: The scenic no-fly route, and it drops you in San Sebastián — the smart base anyway. Book TGV Lyria and SNCF legs when sales open (~4 months out).

gettingThere.route: Zürich → Lyon → Toulouse → Pau → Pamplona via A64/AP-15 (~1,200 km)
gettingThere.duration: 11–12h driving, best split with an overnight in Toulouse or the Pyrenees
gettingThere.notes: Only worth it if you're touring Basque Country/Pyrenees afterwards. Parking in Pamplona during the fiesta is grim — park at the free perimeter lots and walk/shuttle in.

costs.notes: Lodging is the whole story: Pamplona hotels charge 3–5x normal rates and sell out ~9 months ahead. Thrifty = base in San Sebastián or Estella and bus in (or the official campsite), budget flight, street food. Midrange = 3 nights San Sebastián pensión + bullfight ticket + one balcony rental. Splurge = hotel on the encierro route (€400–600/night), private balcony each morning (€100–200/person), barrera bullfight seats.

booking.label: Bullfight tickets — Feria del Toro (Casa de Misericordia, official)

weather: Classic Navarrese July: warm, dry days of 26–29°C, but mornings at the 8am encierro can be a crisp 13–16°C, and a stray Atlantic shower is possible. Pack a cheap all-white outfit plus red pañuelo and faja (buy locally, ~€20 — it will be sangría-stained by July 6 afternoon), a light layer for dawn, and grippy closed shoes for wine-slicked cobbles.
tips: Book lodging 6–12 months out — or skip the Pamplona premium entirely and base in San Sebastián (1h–1h15 by bus; extra-early services run during the fiesta so you can make the 8am encierro).

To watch the run: rent a private balcony on Estafeta or Mercaderes (€100–200, book via agencies months ahead), or grab a cheap ticket into the bullring to see the runners and herd pour in — the double fence on the street fills by 6:30am and views are poor.

If you run: you must be 18+, sober, and inside the course before ~7:30am. Never touch the bulls, never re-enter the course, and if you fall, stay down and cover your head — pile-ups injure far more people than horns. Watch a run first; run 'your' 50 metres, not the whole course.
Skip the crush at the chupinazo in the tiny town hall square unless you enjoy being drenched in sangría shoulder-to-shoulder — Plaza del Castillo has big screens and room to breathe.

Carry cash for peña bars and street stalls, keep phone/wallet in a zipped front pocket (pickpocketing peaks during the fiesta), and note the city essentially doesn't sleep — earplugs if your room is anywhere near the Casco Viejo.

Bullfight tickets are 90% subscription-held; the Feria del Toro releases a small daily allotment and scalping is rife — buy only at the official taquillas or feriadeltoro.com. Sunday July 11, 2027 will be the single busiest day; the quieter final days (12–14) are locals' favourites.

localRecs.name: Café Iruña
localRecs.note: Grand 1888 mirrored café on Plaza del Castillo and Hemingway's actual haunt — have a vermouth where The Sun Also Rises was set; there's a life-size bronze of him at the back bar.

localRecs.name: Bar Gaucho
localRecs.note: Multiple-award-winning pintxos two minutes from Plaza del Castillo — order the huevo trufado and foie with apple; stand-up counter culture at its best.

localRecs.name: Calle Estafeta
localRecs.note: The encierro's famous 300m straight, lined wall-to-wall with bars and pañuelo shops — terrifying at 8am, one long street party by noon.

localRecs.name: Rodero
localRecs.note: Michelin-starred Navarrese fine dining steps from the bullring — the civilised counterpoint to the street chaos; book well ahead for fiesta week.

localRecs.name: Ciudadela and city walls
localRecs.note: Pamplona's star-shaped 16th-century fortress and Europe's best-preserved renaissance walls — a green, quiet recovery walk, and the fireworks are launched here at 11pm nightly.

photos.caption: The first encierro of the fiesta: bulls and runners charge through Pamplona's old town at 8am sharp.
photos.credit: Wikimedia Commons / San Fermin Pamplona Navarra (CC0)

photos.caption: The chupinazo — a sea of white and red erupts as the opening rocket fires from the town hall at noon on July 6.
photos.credit: Wikimedia Commons / San Fermin Pamplona Navarra (CC0)

photos.caption: Runners take their chances ahead of the herd on Calle Estafeta, the encierro's famous long straight.
